 D-64069, Rev. C

June 20,2015

1

Product Identification and Software Version

This document describes the specification of one of the data products generated by the OCO-2 Science Data Operations System (SDOS).   This document applies to the 
following product and system version:

Product Name:
Short Name:
SDOS System Version:
GES DISC Version:
Product Description:

Level 2 Standard Product
OCO2_L2_Standard
B7.0.00 and later
Version 7 and Version 7r and later 
Geolocated retrieved state vectors with CO2-column averaged dry air mole fraction using 3-band full physics retrieval (includes Full-
physics algorithm results that provide details of algorithm performance)

SDOS System Bn.0.00 generates products either with predictive calibration parameters (Version n) or with parameters derived via analysis of past instrument performance 
(Version nr).  Therefore, nr is considered of higher quality.  Analyses mixing the two sets should be undertaken with a degree of caution.  The product specifications for n and 
nr are identical and this document applies to both.  

2

Background information

The OCO-2 SDOS converts telemetry downloaded from the Observatory into data products that provide comprehensive mission results as well as material for further research 
and investigation. The SDOS generates products from Level 0 through Level 2, some of which are available for distribution to both the scientific community and the general 
public.  All products are available to users of the SDOS computing cluster. 

2.1 NASA Data Levels

The following table provides the definitions of the data levels used in this document.  These definitions are standard within the NASA community

Level
Packet data

Level 0

Level 1A

Level 1B

Level 2

Description
Telemetry  data  stream  as  received  at  the  ground  station,  with  science  and  engineering  data 
embedded
Instrument  science  data  (e.g.,  raw  voltages,  counts)  at  full  resolution,  time  ordered,  with 
duplicates and transmission errors removed
NASA  Level  0  data  that  have  been  located  in  space  and  may  have  been  transformed  (e.g., 
calibrated, rearranged) in a reversible manner and packaged with needed ancillary and auxiliary 
data (e.g., radiances with the calibration equations applied)
Irreversibly  transformed  (e.g.,  resampled,  remapped,  calibrated)  values  of  the  instrument 
measurements (e.g., radiances, magnetic field strength)
Geophysical parameters, generally derived from NASA Level 1 data, and located in space and 
time commensurate with instrument location, pointing, and sampling

The following set of tools can be used to open and examine this HDF-5 product on Linux systems.  Other tools may be available.

4

Product Description

4.1 Format and Size

This product is in HDF-5 format.  For most nominal orbits, the product will use up to 600 megabytes.

4.2 Naming Convention

oco2_[ProductId][Mode]_[Orbit][ModeCounter]_[AcquisitionDate]_[ShortBuildId][CalibType]_[ProductionDateTime].h5

the data.

GL - Sample Glint

ND - Sample Nadir

TG - Sample Target

XS - Sample Transition

The Orbit on which the associated data  nnnnn

This field indicates how many times an

a mode occurs only once,

acquired.

software build

Indicates whether processing used 
predictive or retrospective calibration

The date and time (UTC) that the file 
was produced.

t = ID of scheduled build within a major 
build cycle
uu = ID of incremental or patch build
r = retrospective calibration used

Specification Table

The HDF file structure consists of a large number of Data Elements with values.  These Elements (a.k.a., ‘fields’) may be of various types (e.g., arrays, scalars) and are 
organized into Groups.  Groups are utilized in various ways, such as to combine Elements/values generated by different PGE’s.

a priori surface pressure used by the ABO2 algorithm
Empirically determined value to be added to the A-band 
retrieved surface pressure, such that their sum is unbiased in 
clear scenes.  It is modeled as a piecewise linear function of 
solar zenith angle; separate functions are used for land vs. 
ocean pixels.

The value of surface_pressure_abp minus 
surface_pressure_apriori_abp and surface_pressure_offset_abp

retrieved by the ABO2 algorithm

Fraction of continuum level radiance explained by an additive 
offset term in the 757nm spectral window (unitless). In the 
absence of instrumental errors, this will be only caused by 
fluorescence. Rotational Raman scattering should be negligible 
over typical vegetated surface and moderate solar zenith angles 
(<65 degrees).
Fraction of continuum level radiance explained by an additive 
offset term in the 771nm spectral window (unitless). In the 
absence of instrumental errors, this will be only caused by 
fluorescence. Rotational Raman scattering should be negligible 
over typical vegetated surface and moderate solar zenith angles 
(<65 degrees).

6 Appendix 1:  Shape Descriptions

The shape name of a data element is a descriptive label that describes the rank and dimensions of that element. 

Rules for creating shapes:

1.

Shape names do not include any context information, such as what mode the instrument is in when it takes data with that shape.  Any context information needed to 
distinguish between similarly named dimensions is appended as a label, just before the "_Array" suffix.
Any "temporal" dimension, e.g. Frame, is always outermost.
If Frame and Sounding are both present, they occur in direct sequence, i.e. Frame_Sounding_.
Shapes that include Frame, Sounding, and Spectrum cannot have any additional dimensions.
Spectrum precedes all other physical instrument dimensions, except when this rule contradicts any of the above rules.
If Spectrum and Sounding are present in the absence of Frame, they occur in direct sequence, i.e., Spectrum_Sounding.

2.
3.
4.
5.
6.
7. Color comes after SinglePixel.
SinglePixel comes after Slice.
